that you run against your prompt to verify it works consistently.
Think of it as building a
, just like you would for code.
Prompt Engineering Is Iterative, Not Creative
One of the most common mistakes beginners make is trying to write the "perfect prompt" in one sitting. Prompt engineering is not a creative writing exercise; it is an Iterative Engineering Process.
You ask the model a question and see if the answer "feels" right (vibes-based testing)
You have a rigorous, data-driven workflow
LLMs are probabilistic. Just because a prompt works once doesn't mean it works every time. You need a "Test Suite" of edge cases to verify that fixing one problem doesn't break something else.
Transforms Prompt Engineering into Engineering Practice
By adopting a systematic approach to prompt iteration and testing, you transform prompt engineering from an art into a more disciplined engineering practice.
Types of Test Cases to Build
Create a diverse set of test inputs:
that test the boundaries of normal usage.
To build a reliable product, you need a "Golden Dataset"—a collection of inputs and the expected outputs.
JSON with fields: summary, sentiment, confidence
Generate edge cases, adversarial inputs, and golden examples
are ideal outputs that represent the perfect response for a given input.

golden_example = {
    "input": "What's my account balance?",
    "golden_output": """
    To check your balance, I need your Account ID.
    
    Please provide your 10-digit Account ID and I'll retrieve your balance information.
    """
}
Takes a prompt requirement and runs a structured engineering sprint: requirement analysis, prompt design with separation of concerns, test case generation, evaluation loop, and optimization.

The sprint generates 20 test cases (including edge cases like incomplete data), runs 3 eval iterations improving accuracy from 72% to 91%, and documents the final prompt with 4 known limitation scenarios.

1. Requirement Analysis
   → Define inputs, outputs, constraints, quality criteria

2. Prompt Design
   → Draft prompt with upstream/downstream separation and role clarity

3. Test Case Generation
   → Generate edge cases, adversarial inputs, and golden examples

4. Eval Loop
   → Run prompt against test cases, score outputs, iterate on failures

5. Documentation
   → Document the prompt, its constraints, and known limitations
1. Run the prompt with test input
Here's a simple conceptual Python script for automated prompt testing:
Run this script every time you change your system prompt. If your "Pass Rate" drops, your change was a mistake.
Run the prompt with test input → Check output against success criteria → Score pass/fail → Record failures

{
    "test_id": "TC-001",
    "input": "What's my balance?",
    "expected": "Ask for Account ID",
    "actual": "Your balance is $1,234.56",
    "pass": False,  # Failed: Should ask for Account ID first
    "failure_reason": "Model guessed account instead of requesting ID",
    "category": "happy_path"
}
When a model fails, it usually falls into one of three categories:
The model makes up a fact (Hallucination)
Don't change the prompt instructions; give it better data in the context section
The model follows one rule but ignores another
Move the most important rules to the
The model gives a garbled or incomplete response
Every professional LLM Engineer follows this cycle: Initial Prompt → Test on 10+ Examples → Failure Detected? → Refine → Repeat
I discover edge cases as I go and...
The sprint defines 6 input signals, designs a prompt that separates evidence gathering from scoring, generates 20 test cases (including edge cases like incomplete data)

- Incomplete data
- Malformed inputs
- Unexpected formats
- Adversarial inputs
The Workflow: Every time you change your system prompt, you run the entire Golden Dataset through the model. If your "Pass Rate" drops, your change was a mistake.
Never test on just one or two examples.
Track Progress with a Prompt Changelog

- Version number and date
- What changed and why
- Test results before/after
- Known limitations

is essential for systematic prompt engineering:

— Need test suite to verify consistency
3.
When working with develop test cases, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with develop test cases. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of develop test cases scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
